sections.text: Welcome to English One, Module Eleven — Argumentative Writing and Evidence. Today you learn to build an argument the way a serious writer does. State a claim. Back it with real evidence. Explain the reasoning. Take the other side seriously, and answer it.

Here is the trap most students fall into. They write, school uniforms are bad because I do not like them. That is an opinion in a costume. An argument is a claim plus evidence plus reasoning — and it must survive a reader who disagrees. Saying something louder is not an argument. Proving it is.

Game plan. First, learn the claim-evidence-reasoning structure that holds every body paragraph together. Second, write a real thesis statement — not a topic, not a feeling. Third, take a counterargument seriously and rebut it. Then we pause twice on real writing tasks before mapping the four-to-five paragraph essay.

Every body paragraph follows the same shape. Claim — one sentence that names what this paragraph proves. Evidence — a fact, statistic, quotation, or example from a real source. Reasoning — the sentences where you explain how the evidence proves the claim. Most weak paragraphs skip the reasoning. Evidence does not speak for itself. You make it speak.

Opinion is not argument. Opinion says, I think social media is bad for teens. Argument says, social media platforms should set a sixteen-and-up age limit because peer-reviewed studies link heavy adolescent use to measurable rises in anxiety and disrupted sleep. The argument names what should happen, points at evidence, and could be defended in front of someone who disagrees.

A thesis statement does three jobs in one sentence. It names the issue. It states your position. It previews the reasons. Weak — social media is bad. Strong — social media platforms should require users to be at least sixteen because earlier access harms adolescent mental health, sleep, and attention. That is a claim a reader can argue with — and that you can defend.

Your turn. The topic — should the school day start later for high school students? You see two raw pieces of evidence on the slide — a CDC sleep-need range and an AAP school start-time recommendation. Pause now. Write one claim sentence, then one reasoning sentence that connects each piece of evidence to your claim. Press play when you are ready.

Time. A strong response sounds like this. Claim — Genesis High School should move the first-period bell to eight thirty so that students can get the sleep they need. Reasoning — the CDC says teens need eight to ten hours a night, and the AAP recommends an eight-thirty start because earlier bells force students to lose sleep they cannot make up. Notice the move — you named the source, then explained why it proves the claim.

An argument that ignores the other side is half an argument. The strongest essays take the counterargument seriously, then answer it. The shape is concession, then rebuttal. Concession — admit what is true or reasonable on the other side. Rebuttal — show why your position still holds, using evidence. Phrases like, critics argue, or, some parents worry, then, however, signal the move cleanly.

One more. The counterargument you see — a later start time would interfere with after-school sports and parents' work schedules. Pause now. Write two sentences. First, a one-sentence concession that takes that worry seriously. Second, a one-sentence rebuttal that uses real evidence — for example, districts that have shifted start times. Press play when you are ready.

Time. A strong response sounds like this. Concession — critics are right that an eight-thirty start would force families and coaches to redesign after-school routines. Rebuttal — however, districts in Seattle and Minneapolis have shifted bell times without losing athletic seasons, and follow-up studies show students arrived more alert with no drop in sports participation. The concession buys you credibility. The rebuttal wins back the ground.

Here is the essay you are about to write. Paragraph one — intro and thesis statement. Paragraphs two and three — two body paragraphs, each with a claim, two pieces of cited evidence, and a sentence of reasoning. Paragraph four — counterargument and rebuttal. Paragraph five — conclusion that restates the thesis in new words. When you finish, underline your thesis and circle every piece of evidence. If a paragraph has no circles, it has no evidence.

Recap. Claim, evidence, reasoning — the spine of every body paragraph. A thesis names the issue, states the position, and previews the reasons. Take the counterargument seriously, then rebut it with evidence. Underline the thesis, circle the evidence. No evidence, no argument — only a louder opinion.

How to actually master this. Read the Purdue Online Writing Lab page on argumentative essays — it walks through structure and source use. Watch the Khan Academy lesson on writing a claim and thesis statement. In your Learn Portal dashboard, complete the four-to-five paragraph argumentative essay — thesis underlined, evidence circled, one counterargument paragraph with a rebuttal. Book an advisor check-in if your thesis keeps sounding like an opinion instead of a claim. Next up — Module Twelve, Expository and Informational Writing.
